`Leftmost operand must be of same class as operator function. Use this keyword to implicitly get left operand argument. Operators (), [], -> or any assignment operator must be overloaded as a class member function.   Overloading does not actually add any capabilities to C++. Everything you can do with an overloaded operator you can also do with a function.   However, overloaded operators make your programs easier to write, read, and maintain. -> | << [], -> or any assignment operator must be overloaded as a class member function. Called when   Left operand of binary operator is of this class   Single operand of unary operator is of this class Can have object of different class than operator   class Time   {int hrs, mins;   public:   Time (int h,int m)   {	hrs=h;mins=m;  }   void show()   {	cout<<hrs<<“:”<<mins;  }   void sum(Time t)   {	hrs+=t.hrs;  mins+=t.mins;   }   Time operator + (Time t)   {	Time temp;   temp.hrs=hrs+t.hrs;   temp.mins=mins+t.mins;   return temp;   }   }; int main() { Time t1(10,20), t2(11,30); t1.show(); t2.show(); // t1.sum(t2); t1=t1+ t2; t1.show(); t2.show(); }` What does the code indicate about the leftmost operand of an operator function?

Called when   Left operand of binary operator is of this class   Single operand of unary operator is of this class Can have object of different class than operator   class Time   {   int hrs, mins;   public:   Time (int h,int m)   {	hrs=h;mins=m;  }   void show()   {	cout<<hrs<<“:”<<mins;  }   void sum(Time t)   {	hrs+=t.hrs;  mins+=t.mins;   }   Time operator + (Time t)   {	Time temp;   temp.hrs=hrs+t.hrs;   temp.mins=mins+t.mins;   return temp;   }   }; int main() { Time t1(10,20), t2(11,30); t1.show(); t2.show(); // t1.sum(t2); t1=t1+ t2; // t1.operator + (t2) t1.show(); t2.show(); } hrs 10: 20 mins hrs 11: 30 mins hrs 21: 50 mins hrs 11: 30 mins  What is the requirement for the leftmost operand when overloading an operator as a class member function?`,
